exemplar.title: To what extent has the Wings to Fly scholarship program in Kenya improved the quality of life of its 2010â2016 recipients compared to socioeconomically similar non-recipients in Eldoret town?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately with clear purpose and linkage to SDGs and syllabus
Research question is clearly stated with temporal and spatial limits
Methodology outlines both primary and secondary sources with sampling strategy and bias acknowledgment
weaknesses: âQuality of lifeâ remains undefined and split over two lines, reducing flow
Sentence length and phrasing reduce precision and readability
Justification for sample size lacks statistical rationale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although two strands reach the 5â6 band, the methodology strand is at 3â4 and minor phrasing and operationalisation issues prevent a full band-5 performance. Overall this criterion demonstrates adequacy but falls short of consistent excellence, meriting 4â/â6.

strengths: Demonstrates clear knowledge of HDI, human-capital theory and Kenyan context
Effective use of diverse secondary sources (KNEC, UNESCO)
Terminology (e.g. stratified sampling, Spearmanâs rho) is mostly accurate
weaknesses: Explanation of some concepts (grade deviation calculations) is occasionally confused
Secondary literature is often referenced superficially
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Subject knowledge is sound and terminology use is generally accurate, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band, but superficial referencing and occasional conceptual confusion prevent a higher mark. A score of 3â/â6 reflects clear but not excellent understanding.

strengths: Data application is clearly relevant to the research question
Statistical results (p < 0.05) are well presented and linked back to hypotheses
Evaluation acknowledges sampling bias and response-rate limitations
weaknesses: Assumptions behind t-tests (normality, equal variances) are not discussed
Some calculations appear copied without independent derivation
Argument has minor inconsistencies and over-generalisation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research application, analysis and argument all fit the lower end of the 4â6 band: the student establishes relevance and mostly supports conclusions, but lacks depth in evaluation and in handling statistical assumptions. A mid-band score of 4â/â12 accurately reflects this performance.

strengths: Overall structure is logical with clear sections and numbered headings
Figures and tables are consistently formatted and support the narrative
Word count and candidate information are correctly provided
weaknesses: Table of contents is cramped and misaligned
Some figures lack scale bars or proper legends
Inconsistent font sizes and table borders reduce readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets most criteria for a clearly appropriate layout (3â4 band) but is hindered by formatting inconsistencies and omitted map details. A score of 3â/â4 reflects competent but not flawless presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The data gathered (exam results, income, insurance, diet) are relevant to the RQ and are applied to each hypothesis.  While some reliability issues exist, the research remains clearly connected to the argument, fulfilling the 4-6 band albeit at the lower end because relevance is occasionally weakened by questionable data validity.

Both primary (98 surveys, interviews) and secondary sources (KNEC, UNESCO, EGF) are outlined, sampling strategy is described, and simple statistical tests are planned.  Limitations and potential bias are acknowledged.  However, justification of sample size, details of survey design, and data-handling procedures are only partial and some techniques (e.g. use of online calculators) are simplistic.  The methodology is therefore âmostly completeâ rather than fully appropriate, matching the 3-4 band.

The essay demonstrates sound knowledge of HDI, human-capital theory, Kenyan educational context and basic statistical ideas, supported by relevant literature and data tables.  Nevertheless, explanation of some concepts (e.g. grade deviation calculations) is occasionally confused and secondary literature is often referenced superficially.  This places the work in the âclear though not excellentâ 3-4 band.

Figures, tables and headings are consistently formatted, word count and candidate information are supplied, and overall presentation is neat.  Nonetheless, inconsistent table borders, small font in some graphics and occasional truncation show that layout is not flawless.  Therefore the strand meets, but does not exceed, the 3-4 descriptor.

Key geographical terms (HDI, stratified sampling, Spearmanâs rho) are used mostly correctly and consistently.  There are sporadic errors (e.g. âquality of lifeâ¦ HDI dimensionsâ conflations, mis-typed symbols) but they do not impede understanding.  Overall usage fits the descriptor for âmostly accurateâ terminology.

The student states the topic in the title, reiterates it in the introduction, and links it explicitly to the SDGs and the IB Geography syllabus.  Purpose (to assess quality-of-life change for a defined cohort) is unambiguous and the geographical focus on Eldoret is made clear with maps.  Minor phrasing lapses (e.g. very long sentences) slightly reduce precision, but overall the communication is accurate and purposeful, consistent with the 5-6 band.

The essay follows a hypothesis-led structure and constructs a logical narrative linking education, income and health.  Minor inconsistencies (e.g. contradictory statements about income hypothesis) and a tendency to over-generalise prevent the argument from being fully coherent, but it remains generally effective, meriting the 4-6 band at 4 marks.

The student conducts t-tests and Spearman correlations and interprets p-values, showing an attempt at critical statistical analysis.  However, assumptions behind tests are not discussed, some calculations appear copied rather than independently derived, and evaluation of data limitations is brief.  Conclusions are âmostly supportedâ rather than fully, aligning with the mid-band.

A table of contents, numbered sections, introductionâmethodâanalysisâconclusion flow and appendices demonstrate a clear structure that aids the reader.  Some repetition, very long tables and occasional disorganisation of appendices slightly hinder navigation, so the work fits the lower end of the âclearly appropriateâ band.

The research question is presented verbatim under a dedicated heading, contains clear temporal limits (2010-2016), spatial limits (Eldoret town) and a measurable dependent variable (quality of life).  It therefore shows focus and directly addresses the issue under investigation.  The only minor weakness is that âquality of lifeâ is broad and operationalisation is left to later sections; nevertheless the question meets the descriptor for a focused and effective RQ.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated but the split over two lines disrupts flow and âquality of lifeâ remains undefined. Consider operationalising this term with specific indicators here to improve clarity and focus.
Including the word count at the start shows awareness of formatting requirements. Well done meeting the IB submission standards without exceeding the 4000-word limit.
Figure 1 includes geographic coordinates but lacks a scale bar and legend. Adding these will strengthen spatial presentation and interpretation.
The table of contents is thorough, but page numbers and entries are cramped and misaligned. Adjust column widths or spacing to improve navigability.
The introduction effectively links the research to UN SDG 4 and IB Geography, demonstrating good contextual understanding of quality educationâs role in development.
Sentences here exceed 50 words, reducing readability. Break the paragraph into shorter sentences to sharpen focus and improve clarity.
Population projection is relevant but 2024 data are cited without explaining margin of error. Including source reliability will strengthen understanding.
Figure 3âs axis labels truncate the grade symbol. Increase font size or adjust layout to avoid misinterpretation of data.
Secondary data sources are diverse and appropriate, including KNEC and UNESCO. Good demonstration of subject knowledge and source use.
The rationale for selecting 98 participants is given, but justification of statistical power or confidence level is missing. Include a brief rationale for sample size.
Consider discussing potential bias introduced by using an online random generator and how this impacts sample representativeness.
The description of t-test procedure cites an online calculator but does not discuss test assumptions (normality, equal variances). Address these assumptions to strengthen your analysis.
Figure 4âs pie chart clearly shows completion rates, making comparisons intuitive. Effective use of visuals to support conclusions.
Appendix 7 contains extensive raw data but table borders and fonts are inconsistent, making it hard to follow. Streamline formatting for clarity.
The statistical conclusion is well-presented with p <0.05 clearly justifying rejection of Hâ. The interpretation links back effectively to the hypothesis.
Scatter plot (Figure 7) lacks annotation of Spearmanâs rho or regression line. Including these on the graph would enhance critical analysis.
Analytical discussion on anomalies in the income hypothesis shows critical reflection. Charging you for evaluating program weaknesses in career guidance.
The adaptation source for Figure 1 is noted but the citation format is inconsistent with Harvard style. Ensure uniform referencing format throughout.
Appendix 6 lists interview questions but lacks numbering consistency. Align bullet formatting to match main text lists.
Appendix 8 summarises Spearmanâs calculation but does not explain how Î£dÂ² leads to Ï=0.795. Include formula and intermediate steps.
Figures 9 and 10 use similar layouts but legends overlap small chart areas. Separate legend boxes to improve readability.
The conclusion reiterates key findings and links back to the RQ, but consider restating the RQ explicitly and summarising each hypothesis outcome for stronger closure.
Evaluation section acknowledges stratified sampling bias and low interview response. Well presented reflection on methodology limitations.
The evaluation remains largely descriptive. Deepen your reflection by analysing how these limitations could systematically bias your results.
References list shows breadth of sources, but capitalization and italics are inconsistent. Use a consistent referencing style guide.
